calls.label: diagnose
calls.system:
You are an expert tutor. Given a learner's current state and a learning graph,
identify the single concept the learner should focus on NEXT.

Decision rules:
  1. If the learner has unresolved prerequisite gaps, target the deepest
     unresolved prerequisite first.
  2. Otherwise, target the lowest-difficulty unmastered concept whose
     prerequisites are all mastered.
  3. If a struggling concept exists, target it ONLY if its prerequisites are
     mastered — otherwise resolve the prerequisite first.

Set suggested_difficulty to one of:
  - the learner's current difficulty_level (steady)
  - difficulty_level - 1 (if learner is struggling with multiple concepts)
  - difficulty_level + 1 (if learner just mastered an adjacent concept at the
    current level)

Confidence reflects how clearly the rules point at one target (1.0 = unambiguous,
0.5 = several plausible targets, < 0.5 = guessing).


Respond with valid JSON only, conforming to this schema:
{
  "properties": {
    "target_concept_id": {
      "title": "Target Concept Id",
      "type": "string"
    },
    "rationale": {
      "title": "Rationale",
      "type": "string"
    },
    "confidence": {
      "maximum": 1.0,
      "minimum": 0.0,
      "title": "Confidence",
      "type": "number"
    },
    "suggested_difficulty": {
      "maximum": 5,
      "minimum": 1,
      "title": "Suggested Difficulty",
      "type": "integer"
    },
    "prerequisite_gaps": {
      "items": {
        "type": "string"
      },
      "title": "Prerequisite Gaps",
      "type": "array"
    }
  },
  "required": [
    "target_concept_id",
    "rationale",
    "confidence",
    "suggested_difficulty"
  ],
  "title": "GapEstimate",
  "type": "object"
}

Do not include any prose, markdown fences, or commentary outside the JSON.
calls.response_text: {"target_concept_id":"tokens","rationale":"The learner has no mastered concepts and an empty history. Tokens is the only concept with no prerequisites and the lowest difficulty (1), making it the foundational starting point for the entire domain.","confidence":1.0,"suggested_difficulty":1,"prerequisite_gaps":[]}

parsed.rationale: The learner has no mastered concepts and an empty history. Tokens is the only concept with no prerequisites and the lowest difficulty (1), making it the foundational starting point for the entire domain.

calls.label: plan
calls.thinking: Since the learner hasn't mastered tokens yet and has no prior knowledge, I'll structure a progression starting with reading to introduce the concept, then move into elaboration that connects tokens to practical concerns like cost and latency, and finally wrap up with interactive practice where they estimate token counts themselves.

steps.objective: Introduce tokens as subword units from a fixed vocabulary, connecting to the everyday experience of typing text into a chatbot.
Show a worked example: tokenize a sample sentence and walk through why tokens (not words or characters) are the unit of cost, context window, and latency, using the ~4-characters-per-token heuristic.
Have the learner estimate the token count of several short texts and predict relative cost, applying the heuristic to new examples.
Retrieval check: ask the learner to explain from memory why a longer prompt costs more and is slower, reinforcing the cost/latency link without re-reading.

parsed.rationale: Tokens is brand-new expository material for a learner with an empty history and an explicit reading preference, so the KEY teaching steps (1-2) use reading to lay down clean conceptual foundations — this matches the concept's own reading modality_hint. Since tokens has no prerequisites, spaced-repetition on a prior concept isn't possible, so I open with elaboration (anchoring to prior chatbot experience) and a worked_example to model tokenization concretely. Steps 3-4 then shift to interactive retrieval practice (desirable_difficulty) so the learner actively applies the cost/context/latency framing rather than just re-reading, cementing the foundational concept that gates the entire domain.

calls.response_text:
{"type":"reading","title":"What Are Tokens?","body":"When you type a message into a chatbot, it might look like the computer reads your words just like you do. But behind the scenes, something interesting happens first: your text gets chopped into small pieces called tokens.

A token is a chunk of text from a fixed list, called a vocabulary, that the AI knows in advance. A token can be a whole short word like "cat," a piece of a longer word, or even just a space or punctuation mark. For example, the word "unbelievable" might be split into three tokens: "un," "believ," and "able." Common words usually stay whole, while rare or long words get broken into parts.

Think of it like building with LEGO bricks. You don't have a custom brick for every possible shape. Instead, you have a fixed box of brick types, and you snap them together to build anything. Tokens are the AI's bricks for building language. Every sentence you type, and every sentence the AI replies with, is made by snapping these tokens together.

A handy rule of thumb: in English, one token is roughly 4 characters, or about three-quarters of a word. So a short paragraph of 100 words is around 130 tokens. You don't need to count exactly — just know that more text means more tokens.

Why does this matter to you? Tokens are the basic unit the AI uses to measure three important things:

1. Cost: Many AI tools charge based on how many tokens you send and receive. More tokens, more cost.

2. Memory (the context window): An AI can only "hold in mind" a certain number of tokens at once. A very long conversation can eventually run past this limit, and the oldest parts get forgotten.

3. Speed (latency): The AI generates its answer one token at a time. A longer reply simply takes more time to produce.

So the next time you chat with an AI, picture your words being snapped apart into little tokens, processed, and then new tokens being snapped together to form the reply. Understanding this helps you write clearer, shorter prompts and gives you a feel for why long documents cost more and take longer. Tokens are the hidden currency of every AI conversation.","key_takeaways":["A token is a small chunk of text — a word or part of a word — from the AI's fixed vocabulary.","A rough rule: 1 token ≈ 4 English characters, or about ¾ of a word.","Tokens are the unit of cost, the context window (memory), and latency (speed).","Common words become single tokens; rare or long words get split into several.","Shorter text means fewer tokens, which usually means lower cost and faster replies."]}

parsed.title: What Are Tokens?
parsed.body:
When you type a message into a chatbot, it might look like the computer reads your words just like you do. But behind the scenes, something interesting happens first: your text gets chopped into small pieces called tokens.

A token is a chunk of text from a fixed list, called a vocabulary, that the AI knows in advance. A token can be a whole short word like "cat," a piece of a longer word, or even just a space or punctuation mark. For example, the word "unbelievable" might be split into three tokens: "un," "believ," and "able." Common words usually stay whole, while rare or long words get broken into parts.

Think of it like building with LEGO bricks. You don't have a custom brick for every possible shape. Instead, you have a fixed box of brick types, and you snap them together to build anything. Tokens are the AI's bricks for building language. Every sentence you type, and every sentence the AI replies with, is made by snapping these tokens together.

A handy rule of thumb: in English, one token is roughly 4 characters, or about three-quarters of a word. So a short paragraph of 100 words is around 130 tokens. You don't need to count exactly — just know that more text means more tokens.

Why does this matter to you? Tokens are the basic unit the AI uses to measure three important things:

1. Cost: Many AI tools charge based on how many tokens you send and receive. More tokens, more cost.

2. Memory (the context window): An AI can only "hold in mind" a certain number of tokens at once. A very long conversation can eventually run past this limit, and the oldest parts get forgotten.

3. Speed (latency): The AI generates its answer one token at a time. A longer reply simply takes more time to produce.

So the next time you chat with an AI, picture your words being snapped apart into little tokens, processed, and then new tokens being snapped together to form the reply. Understanding this helps you write clearer, shorter prompts and gives you a feel for why long documents cost more and take longer. Tokens are the hidden currency of every AI conversation.
key_takeaways: A token is a small chunk of text — a word or part of a word — from the AI's fixed vocabulary.
A rough rule: 1 token ≈ 4 English characters, or about ¾ of a word.
Tokens are the unit of cost, the context window (memory), and latency (speed).
Common words become single tokens; rare or long words get split into several.
Shorter text means fewer tokens, which usually means lower cost and faster replies.
